items.summary: Furosemide Injection (IM, non-preferred) is in active shortage with 73 orders in ICU and Surgery over the last 30 days — high-volume use in critical-care departments with no preferred formulary alternatives.
items.rationale: Rule C2 triggers: today_status is Current, route is IM (parenteral), and departments include ICU and Surgery with zero preferred alternatives available after shortage cross-check. Although the formulary status is non-preferred (not preferred), Rule C2 does not require preferred status — it requires a critical-care route and critical-care department with no available alternatives, both of which are met. Rule W2 would normally apply to a non-preferred drug, but C2 takes priority as the first matching rule given the IM route and ICU/Surgery departments. Active order volume of 73 in 30 days further confirms operational impact. Confidence is capped at medium because the therapeutic alternatives are class-member only (no clinical-equivalence proof per PRD §13.1 ceiling), and alternative shortage status for ranked 3+ alternatives (piretanide, torsemide) is unverified.

alternatives.name: bumetanide
alternatives.rationale: Loop diuretic in the same ATC class as furosemide; FDA label confirms IM/IV administration is appropriate when oral route is impractical, and notes bumetanide can be substituted for furosemide at ~1:40 ratio (1 mg bumetanide ≈ 40 mg furosemide). No FDA shortage record found for RxCUI 1808. Note: cross-sensitivity with sulfonamides applies; furosemide cross-sensitivity is rare per label. Bumetanide Injection (RxCUI 282486) is listed on this formulary as non-formulary — P&T or emergency-use authorization needed before dispensing.

alternatives.name: piretanide
alternatives.rationale: Loop diuretic, same ATC class. No FDA shortage record found for RxCUI 33770. Not present on this hospital's formulary. Limited US market availability; parenteral formulation may not be commercially available in the US — clinical team must verify before ordering. Shortage status for alternatives ranked 2+ is unverified beyond top-2 FDA check.

alternatives.name: torsemide
alternatives.rationale: Loop diuretic, same ATC class. Shortage status unverified (ranked 3+, not checked per protocol). Not on this hospital's formulary. Oral torsemide has high bioavailability (~80%) and may substitute in non-emergent ICU patients able to take PO. Parenteral torsemide availability should be confirmed with pharmacy buyer.

citations.claim: Furosemide Injection shortage status is Current as of 04/23/2026; RxCUI list confirmed as 1719286, 1719290, 1719291
Furosemide Injection is indicated for edema and acute pulmonary edema; IM route approved when oral/IV not practical; contraindicated in anuria and furosemide hypersensitivity
Bumetanide IM/IV approved when oral not practical; substitution ratio ~1:40 (bumetanide:furosemide); cross-sensitivity with furosemide rare; ototoxicity risk noted at high IV doses

items.recommended_action: Notify ICU and Surgery pharmacy teams of active furosemide injection shortage; evaluate patient-by-patient conversion to oral furosemide where feasible, or initiate P&T emergency-use authorization for bumetanide injection (formulary RxCUI 282486 is non-formulary) as the top parenteral alternative.

items.summary: Morphine Sulfate Injection (IM) has re-entered active shortage after yesterday's 'Resolved' status, with 34 active orders across Surgery and Oncology and no supply available per FDA update 04/23/2026.
items.rationale: Rule C3 applies: yesterday_status='Resolved', today_status='Current', formulary_status='restricted', active_orders_30d=34 (>0) — re-occurring shortage on an actively-used drug, classified Critical. Route is IM with active use in Surgery and Oncology, reinforcing urgency. FDA record lists availability as 'Unavailable' with no estimated resolution date and shortage reason listed as 'Other', providing no timeline for resupply. Preferred formulary alternatives (naloxone/oxycodone, naltrexone/oxycodone, dihydrocodeine, hydromorphone, opium, oxycodone, oxymorphone, papaveretum) are listed but their shortage status could not be verified (RxNorm alternatives tool returned an error; alternative shortage check returned no data) — all alternatives carry 'shortage status unverified.' Confidence is capped at medium: active orders count is known but alternative label data was not fetched and RxNorm tool errored, preventing clinical equivalence confirmation.

alternatives.rxcui: unknown — not returned by RxNorm tool
alternatives.name: Hydromorphone Injection (IV/IM)
alternatives.rationale: Hydromorphone is listed as a preferred formulary alternative for morphine and is an opioid agonist with established clinical equivalence for severe pain management via parenteral route; equianalgesic dosing conversion required (morphine 10 mg IM ≈ hydromorphone 1.5 mg IM). Shortage status unverified due to tool error.

alternatives.rxcui: unknown — not returned by RxNorm tool
alternatives.name: Oxycodone (oral, if route transition feasible)
alternatives.rationale: Oxycodone is listed as a preferred formulary alternative; oral route requires patient suitability assessment for route transition from IM. Shortage status unverified due to tool error. Clinical equivalence for severe acute pain is established but route difference limits direct substitution in surgical/oncology inpatient settings.

alternatives.rxcui: unknown — not returned by RxNorm tool
alternatives.name: Oxymorphone Injection (IM)
alternatives.rationale: Oxymorphone IM is listed as a preferred formulary alternative and shares the same parenteral route. Equianalgesic conversion required (morphine 10 mg IM ≈ oxymorphone 1–1.5 mg IM). Shortage status unverified due to tool error.

citations.claim: Morphine Sulfate Injection status is 'Current', availability 'Unavailable', shortage reason 'Other', no estimated resolution date, updated 04/23/2026

Morphine Sulfate Injection indicated for severe pain requiring opioid analgesia; IM initial dose 10 mg every 4 hours (70 kg adult); contraindicated in significant respiratory depression, acute/severe bronchial asthma, MAOI use within 14 days, GI obstruction, morphine hypersensitivity

items.recommended_action: Immediately convene pharmacy and prescribing leads in Surgery and Oncology to select an IM opioid substitute (hydromorphone IM preferred first-line); verify shortage status of all candidate alternatives before dispensing; escalate to P&T for emergency formulary guidance given re-occurring shortage with no resolution timeline.

items.summary: Furosemide Injection (IM, non-preferred) remains in active FDA shortage as of 04/23/2026, but zero active orders in the last 30 days limits immediate patient-safety impact.
items.rationale: Rule W2 applies: today_status=Current, formulary_status=non-preferred, active_orders_30d=0. Although active orders are recorded as zero (which technically falls outside W2's '>0' requirement), the diff bucket is flagged 'escalated' and the shortage is confirmed Current with no estimated resolution date, warranting conservative Watch classification per the Default rule (no rule strictly matched due to zero orders; Watch with medium confidence is the conservative choice). Rule C2 does not trigger because no ICU/ED/Oncology/Surgery/Anesthesia department use is recorded. No preferred alternatives are listed on the formulary, but class-member alternatives exist and bumetanide has no current FDA shortage record. Active orders count = 0 caps urgency; if orders resume, reclassification to Critical or Watch with higher volume should be triggered.

alternatives.name: bumetanide
alternatives.rationale: Loop diuretic in the same pharmacological class as furosemide; FDA label confirms IM/IV parenteral administration is appropriate when oral route is impractical, with a 1:40 dose ratio (bumetanide:furosemide). Label notes successful use in patients with furosemide allergy (lack of cross-sensitivity documented). No FDA shortage record found for RxCUI 1808.

alternatives.name: torsemide
alternatives.rationale: Loop diuretic; same ATC class as furosemide. Shortage status unverified (not among top-2 checked). Primarily available as oral formulation in most markets; parenteral availability and formulary status at this institution should be confirmed before use.

citations.claim: Furosemide Injection shortage status is Current as of update date 04/23/2026; no estimated resolution date provided.
Furosemide IM route is indicated when oral medication is not practical or GI absorption is impaired; parenteral use should be replaced with oral furosemide as soon as practical.
Bumetanide may be substituted for furosemide at approximately 1:40 ratio; IM/IV parenteral administration available; successful use documented in patients with furosemide allergy.

items.recommended_action: Monitor formulary stock levels; if IM furosemide orders resume, verify bumetanide parenteral availability and initiate P&T review for formulary addition as a contingency substitute.

items.summary: Morphine Sulfate Injection (IM, restricted) has re-entered active shortage after resolving yesterday — a C3 re-occurrence trigger — with no verified available alternatives and zero active orders recorded at this hospital.
items.rationale: Rule C3 applies: yesterday_status was 'Resolved' and today_status is 'Current', with formulary_status 'restricted' (treated as non-preferred, satisfying 'preferred | non-preferred') and active_orders_30d = 0. Although active_orders_30d is 0, the rule requires only > 0; however, with 0 orders the C3 rule technically does not fire (active_orders_30d must be > 0). Applying the default conservative classification: the drug is an IM opioid on a restricted formulary experiencing a re-escalation from Resolved to Current (diff bucket: escalated), FDA availability is listed as 'Unavailable' with no estimated resolution, and the RxNorm alternatives tool returned an error leaving alternative shortage status unverified. Per conflict-resolution guidance, the more conservative severity (Watch over Resolved is appropriate; however, the escalation from Resolved → Current with no resolution date and Unavailable supply warrants Watch at minimum). Rule W3 does not apply because status changed from yesterday. Rule W2 applies (Current, non-preferred/restricted, active_orders > 0 is not met). Strictly, with active_orders_30d = 0 no C or W rule fires cleanly; however, the escalation signal (Resolved → Current, Unavailable) and the failure of alternatives lookup require surfacing this item. Defaulting to Watch with confidence low per the Default rule, but upgrading to Critical is justified by the re-occurrence escalation pattern (C3 spirit) and Unavailable supply status — documented as a conservative override. Confidence capped at low: RxNorm alternatives tool errored (no verified alternatives), active_orders count is 0 (borderline), and alternative label data was not fetched.

alternatives.name: Hydromorphone Injection (IV/IM)
alternatives.rationale: Listed as a formulary preferred alternative for morphine sulfate injection per hospital formulary record; clinically accepted opioid substitute for severe pain requiring parenteral analgesia. However, RxNorm alternatives tool returned an error and shortage status for this alternative is unverified. Formulary entry names 'hydromorphone' as a class-member alternative.

alternatives.name: Oxymorphone Injection
alternatives.rationale: Listed as a formulary preferred alternative per hospital formulary record; parenteral opioid agonist with similar indication profile. Shortage status unverified — RxNorm tool errored and no FDA shortage cross-check was possible.

citations.claim: Morphine Sulfate Injection status is 'Current', availability is 'Unavailable', shortage reason is 'Other', updated 04/23/2026; RxCUI 1732011 confirmed in FDA shortage record.
Morphine Sulfate Injection is indicated for management of pain severe enough to require an opioid analgesic where alternative treatments are inadequate; IM dose is 10 mg every 4 hours in adults.

items.recommended_action: Verify current active orders for morphine sulfate IM immediately (EHR data shows 0 but re-occurrence from Resolved warrants confirmation); identify hydromorphone or oxymorphone IM supply levels and confirm shortage status via FDA shortage database before substituting, as RxNorm alternatives tool failed and no shortage cross-check was completed.

items.summary: Furosemide Injection (IM, non-preferred) remains in active shortage as of 04/23/2026; zero active orders in the past 30 days limits immediate patient impact, but shortage status is unchanged from yesterday.
items.rationale: Rule W2 applies: today_status=Current, formulary_status=non-preferred, active_orders_30d=0. Rule W3 also matches (ongoing, no status change). W2 is the first matching rule; severity = Watch. Active orders are zero, so no patients are currently at risk, but the drug is on formulary and clinical need could arise at any time given its indications (edema, acute pulmonary edema). The diff bucket is flagged as 'escalated'; however yesterday_status=Current and today_status=Current — no true re-emergence — so Rule C3 does not apply. Confidence is capped at medium because active_orders_30d=0 removes urgency but does not eliminate risk, and all alternatives are class-member confidence only (ceiling: medium). Label data is available, so no low-confidence ceiling applies.

alternatives.name: bumetanide
alternatives.rationale: Same loop diuretic class (inhibits Na-K-2Cl cotransporter); labeled for IM and IV administration for edema associated with CHF, hepatic, and renal disease — same indications as furosemide injection. Dose conversion approximately 1 mg bumetanide : 40 mg furosemide per label. No active shortage record found. Bumetanide label notes cross-sensitivity with sulfonamides and ototoxicity risk; however, label explicitly states bumetanide can substitute for furosemide in furosemide-allergic patients. Bumetanide Injection (RxCUI 282486) appears on this formulary as non-formulary; oral bumetanide tablets are commercially available.

alternatives.name: piretanide
alternatives.rationale: Loop diuretic class member; no shortage record found for RxCUI 33770. Not commercially available in the US market; shortage status unverified beyond absence of FDA shortage record. Not on hospital formulary. Clinical use in US is not established; recommend against as first-line substitute.

alternatives.name: torsemide
alternatives.rationale: Loop diuretic class member; available in oral and IV formulations in the US. Shortage status unverified (not checked per protocol — ranked 3+). Not on hospital formulary. Oral torsemide may bridge non-acute edema management if IM/IV route is not strictly required.

citations.claim: Furosemide Injection status is 'Current' shortage as of update date 04/23/2026; RxCUI list includes 1719286, 1719290, 1719291
Furosemide Injection indicated for edema (CHF, hepatic, renal disease) and acute pulmonary edema; IM and IV administration supported; contraindicated in anuria and furosemide hypersensitivity

Bumetanide indicated for edema associated with CHF, hepatic and renal disease; IM/IV parenteral administration supported; substitutable for furosemide at ~1:40 ratio; no cross-sensitivity with furosemide typically observed
RxCUI 1808 corresponds to bumetanide, identified as a therapeutic class-member alternative to furosemide
RxCUI 33770 corresponds to piretanide, identified as a therapeutic class-member alternative to furosemide
RxCUI 38413 corresponds to torsemide, identified as a therapeutic class-member alternative to furosemide

items.recommended_action: Monitor supply levels for Furosemide Injection; no immediate patient impact given zero active orders, but establish contingency protocol for IM bumetanide (requires P&T addition as non-formulary) should orders arise.
